en.extract:
The Mexican chickadee (Poecile sclateri) is a small passerine songbird in the family Paridae, the tits and chickadees. It is found in Mexico and the U. S. states of Arizona and New Mexico.


Taxonomy and systematics
The Mexican chickadee was originally described in 1856 as Parus meridionalis. However, that binomial had already been applied to another species, so by the principle of priority Kleinschmidt renamed it Parus sclateri in 1897. It remained in genus Parus for much of the twentieth century. Following a 1996 publication, in 1998 the American Ornithologists' Union reassigned it to Poecile, a genus that had been erected in 1829 and had long been treated as a subgenus of Parus. A 2005 paper used mtDNA cytochrome b sequence data and morphology to agree with that change and suggest it should be universally adopted. BirdLife International's Handbook of the Birds of the World did so in 2016 and the IOC and the Clements taxonomy followed suit by 2018.
The Mexican chickadee has these four subspecies:

P. s. eidos (Peters, JL, 1927)
P. s. garzai (Phillips, AR, 1986)
P. s. sclateri (Kleinschmidt, 1897)
P. s. rayi (Miller, AH & Storer, 1950)


Description
The Mexican chickadee is about 13 cm (5.1 in) long and weighs 9 to 12 g (0.32 to 0.42 oz) The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black head from the level of the eyes up and a black hindneck, both with a faint bluish gloss. The have pure black cheeks, chin, throat, and upper breast, and the rest of their face is white. Their back, scapulars, rump, and uppertail coverts are deep olive gray or mouse gray, with the strongest olive on the rump. Their wings and tail are slate gray with paler gray edges on most flight feathers. Their lower breast and belly are white and their sides, flanks, and undertail coverts paler olive gray than their back. They have a dark brown iris, a black bill, and bluish legs and feet. Juveniles' heads are grayer than adults'. The subspecies differ slightly in size but are not separable in the field. 


Distribution and habitat
The Mexican chickadee has a disjunct distribution. The subspecies are found thus:

P. s. eidos: from extreme southeastern Arizona and extreme southwestern New Mexico south in northern Mexico's Sierra Madre Occidental
P. s. garzai: northeastern Mexico in the southeastern Coahuila - west-central Nuevo León area
P. s. sclateri: western Mexico from southeastern Sinaloa to Puebla and western Veracruz
P. s. rayi: western Mexico from southern Jalisco to Oaxaca
The Mexican chickadee inhabits pine, pine-oak, and spruce/fir forest in the subtropical and tropical zones at elevations between 1,500 and 3,900 m (4,900 and 12,800 ft). In the U. S. it is primarily found in coniferous forest above about 2,100 m (6,900 ft) when breeding. In Mexico it occurs in all three types and is most common above about 2,200 m (7,200 ft).


Behavior


Movement
The Mexican chickadee is a year-round resident in most of its range but in Arizona, New Mexico, and in scattered locations in Mexico some individuals move to lower elevations for the non-breeding season.


Feeding
The Mexican chickadee feeds mostly on adult and larval insects. It forages throughout conifers but tends to forage in the crown and on the periphery of deciduous trees. It typically gleans its food from leaves and twigs, sometimes hanging upside-down to reach it. It sometimes takes it while briefly hovering. It has not been observed caching food. In winter it commonly forages as part of a mixed-species feeding flock that may have as many as 100 members.


Breeding
The Mexican chickadee breeds primarily between early April and mid-July. It nests in cavities, both natural and human-made such as nest boxes, and the species is thought to excavate some cavities. Females build the nest, a cup of soft materials such as hair, moss, and catkins. Clutches of up to nine eggs have been found; the eggs are dull white with small pale reddish brown spots. Only the female incubates the clutch and broods nestlings, but both parents provision young. The incubation period and time to fledging are not known.


Vocalization
The Mexican chickadee's song is "a series of short abrupt phrases peeta peeta peeta". Its principal calls are "a high buzzy sschleeeer" and "a high, buzzy, and hissing tzee tzee shhh shhh".


Status
The IUCN has assessed the Mexican chickadee as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its estimated population of two million mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified. "The long-term future of U.S. populations of Mexican Chickadee and other Sierra Madrean animals may be precarious" due to their limited distribution and discontinuous habitat...Chickadees use a broader range of forest types in Mexico than in the U.S., and the species thus seems less threatened there."


References


External links
Mexican chickadee photo gallery at VIREO (Drexel University)

The '''Mexican chickadee''' ('''''Poecile sclateri''''') is a small [[passerine]] [[songbird]] in the family [[Paridae]], the tits and chickadees.<ref name=IOC15.1>{{cite web |url=https://www.worldbirdnames.org/new/bow/waxwings/ |title=Waxwings and allies, tits, penduline tits |website=IOC World Bird List |version =v 15.1 | editor1-last=Gill | editor1-first=Frank | editor1-link=Frank Gill (ornithologist) | editor2-last=Donsker | editor2-first=David | editor3-last=Rasmussen | editor3-first=Pamela | editor3-link=Pamela Rasmussen |date=March 2025 |access-date=17 December 2025 }}</ref> It is found in Mexico and the U. S. states of [[Arizona]] and [[New Mexico]].<ref name=AOU1998>{{cite book | last =<!--Not stated--> | first =<!--Not stated--> |  title =Check-list of North American Birds | publisher =American Ornithologists' Union |edition =7th | date =1998 | location =Washington, D.C. | pages =465 }}</ref>

==Taxonomy and systematics==

The Mexican chickadee was [[Species description|originally described]] in 1856 as ''Parus meridionalis''.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Sclater |first=Philip Lutley | journal=Proceedings of the Zoological Society of London | title=Catalogue of the Birds collected by M. Auguste Sallé in Southern Mexico, with Descriptions of New Species | volume=XXIV | pages=293 | date= 1856 |language=Latin, English | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/12860843 |access-date=December 22, 2025 }}</ref> However, that [[Binomial nomenclature|binomial]] had already been applied to another species, so by the [[principle of priority]] Kleinschmidt renamed it ''Parus sclateri'' in 1897.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Kleinschmidt |first=O. | journal=Journal für Ornithologie | title=Beiträge zur Ornis des Grossherzogtums Hessen und der Provinz Hessen-Nassau | volume=XLV | pages=133 | publisher=Friedländer | date= 1897 |language=German | url=https://www.biodiversitylibrary.org/page/33166885 | access-date=December 22, 2025 }}</ref> It remained in genus ''[[Parus]]'' for much of the twentieth century. Following a 1996 publication, in 1998 the [[American Ornithological Society|American Ornithologists' Union]] reassigned it to ''[[Poecile]]'', a genus that had been erected in 1829 and had long been treated as a [[subgenus]] of ''Parus''.<ref>Slikas, B., F. H. Sheldon and F. B. Gill. (1996). "Phylogeny of titmice (Paridae): I. Estimate of relationships among subgenera based on DNA-DNA hybridization" ''Journal of Avian Biology'' 27:7-82.</ref><ref name=AOU1998/> A 2005 paper used [[mtDNA]] [[cytochrome b|cytochrome ''b'']] [[DNA sequence|sequence]] data and [[morphology (biology)|morphology]] to agree with that change and suggest it should be universally adopted.<ref>{{cite journal |last=Gill |first=F. B. |last2=Slikas |first2=B. |last3=Sheldon |first3=F. H. |date=2005|title=Phylogeny of titmice (Paridae): II. Species relationships based on sequences of the mitochondrial cytochrome-''b'' gene. |journal=The Auk |volume=122 |issue=1 |pages=121–143 |url=https://academic.oup.com/auk/article/122/1/121/5562449?login=false |access-date=December 22, 2025 }}</ref> [[BirdLife International]]'s ''[[Handbook of the Birds of the World]]'' did so in 2016 and the [[International Ornithologists' Union|IOC]] and the [[Clements taxonomy]] followed suit by 2018.<ref name=HBW2016>BirdLife International (2016) Handbook of the Birds of the World and BirdLife International digital checklist of the birds of the world. [Old] Version 9. Available at: http://datazone.birdlife.org/userfiles/file/Species/Taxonomy/BirdLife_Checklist_Version_90.zip.</ref><ref name=IOC8.1>Gill, F and D Donsker (Eds). 2018. IOC World Bird List (v 8.1). Doi 10.14344/IOC.ML.8.1.</ref><ref name=Clements2018>Clements, J. F., et al. 2018. The eBird/Clements checklist of birds of the world: v2018.</ref>

The Mexican chickadee has these four subspecies:<ref name=IOC15.1/>

*''P. s. eidos'' ([[James L. Peters|Peters, JL]], 1927)
*''P. s. garzai'' ([[Allan Robert Phillips|Phillips, AR]], 1986)
*''P. s. sclateri'' ([[Otto Kleinschmidt|Kleinschmidt]], 1897)
*''P. s. rayi'' ([[Alden H. Miller|Miller, AH]] & [[Robert W. Storer|Storer]], 1950)

==Description==

The Mexican chickadee is about {{convert|13|cm|in|abbr=on}} long<ref name=Sibley>{{cite book | last =Sibley | first =David Allen | author-link =David Allen Sibley | title =The Sibley Guide to Birds of Western North America | publisher =Alfred A. Knopf | date =2003 | location =New York | pages =321 | isbn =0-679-45121-8  }}</ref> and weighs {{convert|9|to|12|g|oz|abbr=on}}<ref name=MECH-BOW>Ficken, M. S. and J. Nocedal (2020). Mexican Chickadee (''Poecile sclateri''), version 1.0. In Birds of the World (A. F. Poole, P. R. Stettenheim, and F. B. Gill, Editors). Cornell Lab of Ornithology, Ithaca, NY, USA. https://doi.org/10.2173/bow.mexchi.01 retrieved December 22, 2025</ref> The sexes have the same plumage. Adults have a black head from the level of the eyes up and a black hindneck, both with a faint bluish gloss. The have pure black cheeks, chin, throat, and upper breast, and the rest of their face is white. Their back, [[Birdgloss#scapulars|scapulars]], rump, and uppertail [[coverts]] are deep olive gray or mouse gray, with the strongest olive on the rump. Their wings and tail are slate gray with paler gray edges on most flight feathers. Their lower breast and belly are white and their sides, flanks, and undertail coverts paler olive gray than their back. They have a dark brown iris, a black bill, and bluish legs and feet. Juveniles' heads are grayer than adults'. The subspecies differ slightly in size but are not separable in the field.<ref name=MECH-BOW/> 

==Distribution and habitat==

The Mexican chickadee has a [[disjunct distribution]]. The subspecies are found thus:<ref name=MECH-BOW/>

*''P. s. eidos'': from extreme southeastern Arizona and extreme southwestern New Mexico south in northern Mexico's [[Sierra Madre Occidental]]<ref name=MECH-BOW/><ref name=AviList2025>AviList Core Team. 2025. AviList: The Global Avian Checklist, v2025. https://doi.org/10.2173/avilist.v2025 retrieved June 11, 2025</ref>
*''P. s. garzai'': northeastern Mexico in the southeastern [[Coahuila]] - west-central [[Nuevo León]] area<ref name=AOU1998/><ref name=AviList2025/>
*''P. s. sclateri'': western Mexico from southeastern [[Sinaloa]] to [[Puebla]] and western [[Veracruz]]<ref name=AviList2025/>
*''P. s. rayi'': western Mexico from southern [[Jalisco]] to [[Oaxaca]]<ref name=AviList2025/>

The Mexican chickadee inhabits pine, [[Pine-oak forest|pine-oak]], and spruce/fir forest in the subtropical and tropical zones at elevations between {{convert|1500|and|3900|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=AOU1998/> In the U. S. it is primarily found in [[coniferous]] forest above about {{convert|2100|m|ft|abbr=on}} when breeding. In Mexico it occurs in all three types and is most common above about {{convert|2200|m|ft|abbr=on}}.<ref name=MECH-BOW/>

==Behavior==
===Movement===

The Mexican chickadee is a year-round resident in most of its range but in Arizona, New Mexico, and in scattered locations in Mexico some individuals move to lower elevations for the non-breeding season.<ref name=AOU1998/><ref name=MECH-BOW/>

===Feeding===

The Mexican chickadee feeds mostly on adult and larval insects. It forages throughout conifers but tends to forage in the crown and on the periphery of deciduous trees. It typically gleans its food from leaves and twigs, sometimes hanging upside-down to reach it. It sometimes takes it while briefly hovering. It has not been observed caching food. In winter it commonly forages as part of a [[mixed-species feeding flock]] that may have as many as 100 members.<ref name=MECH-BOW/>

===Breeding===

The Mexican chickadee breeds primarily between early April and mid-July. It nests in cavities, both natural and human-made such as nest boxes, and the species is thought to excavate some cavities. Females build the nest, a cup of soft materials such as hair, moss, and [[catkin]]s. Clutches of up to nine eggs have been found; the eggs are dull white with small pale reddish brown spots. Only the female incubates the clutch and broods nestlings, but both parents provision young. The incubation period and time to fledging are not known.<ref name=MECH-BOW/>

{{birdsong|url=https://xeno-canto.org/species/Poecile-sclateri |species=the Mexican chickadee}}
===Vocalization===

The Mexican chickadee's song is "a series of short abrupt phrases ''peeta peeta peeta''". Its principal calls are "a high buzzy ''sschleeeer''" and "a high, buzzy, and hissing ''tzee tzee shhh shhh''".<ref name=Sibley/>

==Status==

The [[IUCN]] has assessed the Mexican chickadee as being of Least Concern. It has a very large range; its estimated population of two million mature individuals is believed to be decreasing. No immediate threats have been identified.<ref name=IUCN/> "The long-term future of U.S. populations of Mexican Chickadee and other Sierra Madrean animals may be precarious" due to their limited distribution and discontinuous habitat...Chickadees use a broader range of forest types in Mexico than in the U.S., and the species thus seems less threatened there."<ref name=MECH-BOW/>
